m_Item1.QuestionText: A class declaring another class as a friend will:

Options.OptionText: Have wine and cheese with that other friend.
Allow that class to declare an instance of it in its list of private variables.
Allow the other class (the one declared as friend) to access to the declaring class’ s private variables
Allow the class declaring the other as a friend to access the declared class’ s private variables.

m_Item1.Explanation: A function or class cannot declare itself as a friend of any class. In a class definition, use the friend keyword and the name of a non-member function or other class to grant it access to the private and protected members of your class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does derived class does not inherit from the base class?
m_Item1.Explanation:
A derived class inherits all base class methods with the following exceptions −
  • Constructors, destructors and copy constructors of the base class.
  • Overloaded operators of the base class.
  • The friend functions of the base class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of following C++ code?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
class BaseClass {
      int i;
     public:
     void setInt(int n);
     int getInt();
};
class DerivedClass : public BaseClass {
     int j;
     public:
     void setJ(int n);
     int mul();
};
void BaseClass::setInt(int n) {
     i = n;
}
int BaseClass::getInt() {
     return i;
}
void DerivedClass::setJ(int n) {
     j = n;
}
int DerivedClass::mul() {
     return j * getInt();
}
int main() {
    DerivedClass ob;
    ob.setInt(10);     
    ob.setJ(4);      
    cout << ob.mul();   
    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Derive class object is able to call fucntion from parent class so setint(10) will assign value to " i " variable which is available in base class and setj(4) is use to assign values to variable " j ". so multiply will return 40 value.

class can have more than two base classes
class can have more than two derive classes

m_Item1.Explanation: Multiple Inheritance is a feature of C++ where a class can inherit from more than one classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   #include <iostream>
   using namespace std;
   class Base  {
     public:
     virtual void print() const = 0;
   };
   class DerivedOne : public Base {
     public:    
     void print() const  {
       cout << "DerivedOne ";
     }
   };
   class DerivedTwo : public Base  {
     public:
     void print() const     {
       cout << "DerivedTwo ";
     }    
   }; 
   class Multiple : public DerivedOne, public DerivedTwo    {
     public:
     void print() const  {
       DerivedTwo :: print();
     } 
   }; 
   int main()
   {
     int i;
     Multiple both; 
     DerivedOne one; 
     DerivedTwo two; 
     Base *array[ 3 ]; 
     array[ 0 ] = &both; 
     array[ 1 ] = &one;
     array[ 2 ] = &two;
     array[ i ] -> print();
     return 0;
   }
What does inheriatance allows you to do?
m_Item1.Explanation: In inheritance we are deriving one class from another class which will create hirarchy of classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following C++ code?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
class BaseClass {
     int x;
    public:
     void setx(int n) {
       x = n;
     }
     void showx() {
       cout << x ;
     }
};
class DerivedClass : private BaseClass {
     int y;
     public:
     void setxy(int n, int m) {
       setx(n);   
       y = m;
       x=y;
     }
     void showxy() {
       showx();    
       cout << y << ' ';
     }
};
int main() {
     DerivedClass ob;
     ob.setxy(10, 20);
     ob.showxy();
     return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Note that x is private data member of base class so it can't be accessed directly in derived class. So the line x=y will give error during compilation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does derived class does not inherit from the base class?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Following are the things which a derived class inherits from its parent.
1) Every data member defined in the parent class (although such members may not always be
accessible in the derived class!)
2) Every ordinary member function of the parent class (although such members may not always be
accessible in the derived class!)
3) The same initial data layout as the base class.
Following are the things which a derived class doesn’t inherits from its parent :
1) The base class’s constructors and destructor.
2) The base class’s friends
3)  operator = () members

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   #include <iostream>
   using namespace std;
   class BaseClass    {
     int x;
     public:
     void setx(int n)      {
       x = n;
     }
     void showx()      {
       cout << x ;
     }
   };
   class DerivedClass : BaseClass    {
     int y;
     public:
     void setxy(int n, int m)      {
       setx(n);   
       y = m;
     }
     void showxy()     {
       showx();    
       cout << y << ' ';
     }
   };
   int main()
   {
     DerivedClass ob;
     ob.setxy(10, 20);
     ob.showxy();
     return 0;
   }
m_Item1.Explanation: In setxy method we are calling base class function to set value of x and same in showxy method we are calling base class function to display x hence output is 10 20.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Pick out the correct statement about override.

Options.OptionText: Overriding refers to a derived class function that has the same name and signature as a base class virtual function
Overriding has same name and different signature
Overriding refers to a derived class
Overriding refers to having different return type in derived class

m_Item1.Explanation: Function overriding is a feature that allows us to have a same function in child class which is already present in the parent class. A child class inherits the data members and member functions of parent class, but when you want to override a functionality in the child class then you can use function overriding. It is like creating a new version of an old function, in the child class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following programme?
#include < iostream.h >
#include < conio.h >
class test
{
     int a;
     public:
     ~test() { }
     test(){   a = 10;   }
     test(int p){ a = p; }
     virtual void display()
     {
cout << endl << a;
    }
};
class subtest:public test
{
     int b;
     public:
    subtest() {    b = 20; }
     subtest(int p, int q):test(p) {     b = q;    }
     void display() { cout << endl << a << “” << b; }
};
 
void main()
{
     test *t;
     t = new test(100);
     t->display();
     t = new subtest(20,40);
     t->display();
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Base class Pointer is generic pointer so it can hold address if any derive class object . So first time t=new test(100) will call base class constructor and t->display() will display from base class. t=new subset(20,40) will call constructor from derive class .

m_Item1.Explanation: The benefit of dynamic binding and polymorphism is that they help making the code easier to extend but by multiple inheritance it makes harder to track.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of following is not a member of a class?
m_Item1.Explanation: Friend function, that is a "friend" of a given class, is a function that is given the same access as methods to private and protected data. A friend function is declared by the class that is granting access, so friend functions are part of the class interface, like methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In protected inheritance:

Options.OptionText: The public members of the base class become public.
The public members of the base class become protected.
The protected members of the base class become private.
The public members of the base class become inaccessible.

m_Item1.Explanation:
public Inheritance − When deriving a class from a public base class, public members of the base class become public members of the derived class and protected members of the base class become protected members of the derived class. A base class's private members are never accessible directly from a derived class, but can be accessed through calls to the public and protected members of the base class.

Protected  Inheritance − When deriving from a protected base class, public and protected members of the base class become protected members of the derived class.

Private Inheritance − When deriving from a private base class,  public and  protected  members  of the base class become private members of the derived class.

  }
};
class Derived:public Base1, public Base2 {

    cout << " Derived's destructor" << endl;
  }
};

  return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Destructors are always called in reverse order of constructors. so first derived class object get destroyed and then parent class objects.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;
class ABC {
public:
  void print ()   {
    cout << " Inside ABC";
  }
};
class PQR:public ABC {
public:
  void print ()   {
    cout << " Inside PQR";
  }
};
class LMN:public PQR {
};
int main (void) {
  LMN obj;
  obj.print ();
  return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: The print function is not present in class Derived claa LMN. So it is looked up in the inheritance hierarchy. print() is present in both classes Base classes, which of them should be called? The idea is, if there is multilevel inheritance, then function is linearly searched up in the inheritance hierarchy until a matching function is found. Hence Print function from PQR is called.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
class base {
public:
    virtual void baseFun() {
        cout << "from base" << endl;
    }
};
class deri : public base {
public:
    void baseFun() override {
        cout << "from derived" << endl;
    }
};
void SomeFunc(base *baseObj) {
    baseObj->baseFun();
}
int main() {
    base baseObject;
    SomeFunc(&baseObject); // Calls base::baseFun()

    deri deriObject;
    SomeFunc(&deriObject); // Calls deri::baseFun() due to polymorphism

    return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
When SomeFunc(&baseObject) is called:

baseObject is of type base.
SomeFunc calls baseObject->baseFun(), which calls baseFun() from the base class, printing "from base".
When SomeFunc(&deriObject) is called:

deriObject is of type deri, which is derived from base.
SomeFunc calls deriObject->baseFun(). Due to the virtual function mechanism (polymorphism), deri's baseFun() is called, printing "from derived".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
class Base {
       int x;
public:
       Base(int a) {
              x = a;
       }
       void display() {
              cout << " In base class "<<x;
       }
};
class Derived : Base {
       int y;
public:
       Derived(int a, int b) : Base(a)    {
              y = b;
       }
       void display()  {
              Base::display();
              cout << " In derived class "<<y;
       } 
};
void main()
{
       Derived *d = new Derived(10,20);
       dynamic_cast<Base*>(d)->display();
}
m_Item1.Explanation: default mode of inheritance is Private. Derived(int a,int b): Base(a) indicate mode of inheritance private. so can not access base class is ERROR

m_Item1.QuestionText: When you create an object of a derived class in C++

Options.OptionText: Derived class constructor is called first then the base class constructor
Base class constructor is called first then derived class constructor
base class constructor will not be called

m_Item1.Explanation: First, the base constructor is called. Then, the base-class members are initialized in the order in which they appear in the class declaration. Finally, the derived constructor is called.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following statements are not true about destructor?
1. It is invoked when object goes out of the scope
2. Like constructor, it can also have parameters
3. It can be virtual
4. It can be declared in private section
5. It bears same name as that of the class and precedes Lambda sign
m_Item1.Explanation: Point 1:Destructors are usedto deallocate memory or descope an object,so it is invoked when object goes out of scope thus ,this statement is correct. Point 2:Destructors can neither return a value nor have arguments thus ,this statement is wrong. Point 3:There can be virtual destructors,we just need to add keyword virtual before the destructor name(e.g., virtual ~destruct() ).

Options.OptionText: class name : access specifer class name

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What happens in the case of inheritance where both base and derived class are having constructors, when an
object of derived class is created?

Options.OptionText: constructor of derived class will be invoked first
constructor of base class will be invoked first
constructor of derived class will be executed first followed by base class
constructor of base class will be executed first followed by derived class

m_Item1.Explanation: When an object of a derived class is created, the base class constructor is called first to ensure that the base class part of the object is properly initialized. Only after the base class constructor completes its execution does the derived class constructor get called to initialize the derived class part of the object. This sequence ensures the correct construction of the entire object.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   #include <iostream>
   using namespace std;
   class polygon   {
     protected:
     int width, height;
     public:
     void set_values (int a, int b) { 
       width = a; height = b;
      }
     };
     class output1  {
       public:
         void output (int i);
     };
   void output1::output (int i) 
   {
     cout << i<<””;
   }
   class rectangle: public polygon, public output1 
   {
     public:
     int area ()    { 
       return (width * height); 
     }
   };
   class triangle: public polygon, public output1  {
     public:
     int area ()  {
       return (width * height / 2); 
     }
   };
   int main () 
   {
     rectangle rect;
     triangle trgl;
     rect.set_values (4, 5);
     trgl.set_values (4, 5);
     rect.output (rect.area());
     trgl.output (trgl.area());
     return 0;
   }
m_Item1.Explanation:
Explanation: We are using the multiple inheritance to find the area of rectangle and triangle.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   #include <iostream>
   using namespace std;
   class Base
   {
     public:
     virtual void print() const = 0;
   };
   class DerivedOne : public Base
   {
     public:    
     void print() const
     {
       cout << "DerivedOne ";
     }
   };
   class DerivedTwo : public Base
   {
     public:
     void print() const
     {
       cout << 'DerivedTwo ';
     }    
   }; 
   class Multiple : public DerivedOne, public DerivedTwo
   {
     public:
     void print() const
     {
       DerivedTwo :: print();
     } 
   }; 

   int main()
   {
     int i=1;
     Multiple both; 
     DerivedOne one; 
     DerivedTwo two; 
     Base *array[ 3 ]; 
     array[ 0 ] = &both; 
     array[ 1 ] = &one;
     array[ 2 ] = &two;
    // array[ i ] -> print();
     return 0;
   }
m_Item1.Explanation: This output occurs because array[i] is pointing to the one object of type DerivedOne, and calling the print() method on it prints "DerivedOne ".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
   #include <iostream>
   using namespace std;
   class student   {
     public:
     int rno , m1 , m2 ;
     void get()   {
       rno = 15, m1 = 10, m2 = 10;
     }
   };
   class sports    {
     public:
     int sm;
     void getsm()      {
       sm = 10;
     }
   };
   class statement:public student,public sports    {
     int tot,avg;
     public:
     void display()      {
       tot = (m1 + m2 + sm);
       avg = tot / 3;
       cout << tot;
       cout << avg;
     }
   };
   int main()
   {
     statement obj;
     obj.get();
     obj.getsm();
     obj.display();
   }
m_Item1.Explanation:
obj.get() sets rno = 15, m1 = 10, and m2 = 10.
obj.getsm() sets sm = 10.
obj.display():
tot is calculated as m1 + m2 + sm, which is 10 + 10 + 10 = 30.
avg is calculated as tot / 3, which is 30 / 3 = 10.
tot is printed, which is 30.
avg is printed, which is 10.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a type of inheritance?

Options.OptionText: Multiple
Multilevel
Distributive
Hierarchical

m_Item1.Explanation:
Types of inheritance in cpp 
  1. Single Inheritance
  2. Multiple Inheritance
  3. Multilevel Inheritance
  4. Hierarchical Inheritance
  5. Hybrid (Virtual) Inheritance

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following  provides a reuse mechanism?

Options.OptionText: Abstraction
Inheritance
Dynamic binding

m_Item1.Explanation: Inheritance is accruing property from one class to another class so, reusing classes is inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following can be facilitated by the Inheritance mechanism?

1.    Use the existing functionality of base class.
2.    Overrride the existing functionality of base class.
3.    Implement new functionality in the derived class.
4.    Implement dynamic polymorphism
5.    Implement containership.
m_Item1.Explanation: Containment is using object of one class as data member of another class. We don't need inheritance for achieving this.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which concept of OOPs is shown by Virtual Functions?
m_Item1.Explanation: Virtual function allows us to give different definitions of the same function i.e. overloading of functions which is known as Polymorphism.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the order of Constructors call when the object of derived class B is declared, provided class B is derived from class A?

Options.OptionText: Constructor of A followed by B
Constructor of B followed by A

m_Item1.Explanation: Firstly the Constructor of class A is called then class B because the Constructor of the base class is called before derived class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be the output of the following C++ code?
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;
class Mammal
{
   public:
    Mammal(){
        cout<<"I'm a Mammal ";
    }
    ~Mammal(){}
};
class Human: public Mammal
{
   public:
    Human(){
        cout<<"I'm a Human ";
    }
    ~Human(){}
};
class Male: public Human
{
   public:
    Male(){
        cout<<"I'm a Male ";
    }
    ~Male(){}
};
class Female: public Human
{
   public:
    Female(){
        cout<<"I'm a Female ";
    }
    ~Female(){}
};
 int main(int argc, char const *argv[])
{
    Male M;
    return 0;
}

Options.OptionText:
I'm a Mammal
I'm a Human
I'm a Male

I'm a Mammal
I'm a Human
I'm a Female

m_Item1.Explanation: As the Male class is derived from Human class and Human class is derived from the Mammal class. Therefore when an object of Male is declared then three constructors will be called namely Mammal(), Human() and Male() in the given order.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output of following code?
#include <iostream>
#include <exception>
using namespace std;
class base { virtual void dummy() {} };
class derived: public base { int a; };
int main ()
 {
     try
     {
            base * pba = new derived;
            base * pbb = new base;
            derived * pd;
            pd = dynamic_cast<derived*>(pba);
            if (pd == 0)
                cout << "Null pointer on first type-cast" << endl;
            pd = dynamic_cast<derived*>(pbb);
            if (pd == 0)
                cout << "Null pointer on second type-cast" << endl;
        }
        catch (exception& e)
        {
            cout << "Exception: " << e.what();
        }
        return 0;
 }
m_Item1.Explanation: In this program, We apply the dynamic cast to pd. Based on the value in the pd, it produces the output.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the keyword used to inherit a class in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, the 'extends' keyword is used after the colon (:) in the derived class declaration to inherit from the base class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which access specifier is typically used to make members private in the base class inaccessible directly in the derived class?
m_Item1.Explanation: When inheritance is specified as 'private', public members of the base class become private members of the derived class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C++, which of the following cannot be inherited?
m_Item1.Explanation: Constructors cannot be inherited in C++ because they are invoked to create new instances of a class and must have the same name as the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What type of inheritance is used if a class inherits from multiple base classes?
m_Item1.Explanation: Multiple Inheritance is when a class can inherit behaviors and features from more than one superclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does the 'virtual' keyword indicate when used in a function declaration in a base class?

Options.OptionText: The function can be overridden in a derived class
The function is only for the base class

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'virtual' keyword is used to allow a function to be overridden in any derived class, enabling polymorphism.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following denotes a derived class with public inheritance?
m_Item1.Explanation: Public inheritance uses the syntax ': public', allowing members of the base class to retain their access levels in the derived class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a base class destructor is not made virtual, what might happen?

Options.OptionText: Objects may not be destroyed properly

m_Item1.Explanation: If a base class destructor is not virtual, derived class destructors may not execute correctly if an object is deleted through a base class pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if a derived class does not override a virtual function in the base class?
m_Item1.Explanation: If a derived class does not override the virtual function, the base class version will be called when an instance of the derived class invokes that function.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which inheritance type results in a diamond problem?
m_Item1.Explanation: The diamond problem is encountered in multiple inheritance when two classes derive from the same base class and a derived class inherits from both.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C++, how do you indicate that a derived class should only inherit from the interface and not directly?
m_Item1.Explanation: To designate a class as an interface, you set its pure virtual functions to 0, meaning the derived class must implement these functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What access level do protected members of a base class have in a derived class?
m_Item1.Explanation: Protected members remain protected in the derived class, allowing them to be accessed by the derived class and its descendants.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is used to access the immediate base class in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, you can use the base class name followed by the scope resolution operator '::' to access members directly from the base class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A derived class constructor calls the constructor of which class first?
m_Item1.Explanation: The base class constructor is always called prior to that of the derived class to ensure the base class components are constructed first.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is achieved if a derived class redefines a non-virtual function of the base class?
m_Item1.Explanation: If the derived class redefines a non-virtual function, the base class function implementation is hidden but not overridden.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a base class function is virtual, what must the derived class function use to override it?
m_Item1.Explanation: There’s no requirement to use any keywords in the derived class function, but 'override' can be used for clarity and intent.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the output if a class derived from a base class doesn’t implement a pure virtual function?
m_Item1.Explanation: A derived class must implement all pure virtual functions; failing to do so results in a compile-time error because the derived class is still abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if an abstract class contains a constructor in C++?

Options.OptionText: Derived class can call the constructor

m_Item1.Explanation: Even though you cannot instantiate an abstract class, its constructor can be called by derived classes when those instances are created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can a derived class access the overridden method of its base class in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The syntax 'base::method()' is used to call the overridden method from a derived class, where 'base' is the name of the class whose method you want to invoke.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about the v-table in C++?

Options.OptionText: It is used with inline functions
It stores names of all members
It is used with virtual functions

m_Item1.Explanation: The v-table (virtual method table) is a mechanism used to support dynamic polymorphism through virtual functions in C++.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How are pure virtual functions characterized?
m_Item1.Explanation: A pure virtual function is declared by assigning 0 in its declaration, making the class abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is correct regarding abstract classes?
m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes can have implementations for some of its methods along with pure virtual methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In what situation is making a destructor virtual necessary?
m_Item1.Explanation: Making destructors virtual ensures that the derived class's destructor is called when deleting an object through a base class pointer.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can a class be prevented from being derived in C++?

Options.OptionText: Declare the class constructor as private

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, there's no native 'sealed' keyword like in other languages, but you can prevent inheritance by making the constructor private or marking it 'final'.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one of the following is NOT a type of inheritance in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: Classical inheritance is not a term used in C++, which deals with single, multiple, multilevel, and hierarchical inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scenarios can lead to shadowing in C++ inheritance?
m_Item1.Explanation: When a derived class member has the same name as a base class member, the base class version gets shadowed or hidden.
